title: Flexibility in C^0 symplectic geometry
speaker: Lev Buhovsky
abstract: Traditionally, objects of study in symplectic geometry are smooth - such as symplectic and Hamiltonian diffeomorphisms, Lagrangian (or more generally, isotropic and co-isotropic) submanifolds etc. However, in the course of development of the field, non-smooth objects or features still appeared - such as the Poincaré-Birkhoff theorem about fixed points of an area-preserving homeomorphism of the 2-dim annulus satisfying the twist condition, or the Eliashberg-Gromov theorem about uniform limits of symplectic diffeomorphisms.
tags: Workshop on the h-principle and beyond

proof_stages.label: C^0 symplectic-homeomorphism category
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines symplectic homeomorphisms as C^0 limits of symplectic diffeomorphisms, recalls the Gromov--Eliashberg smooth-rigidity theorem, and emphasizes the major unresolved fragmentation obstruction in dimensions greater than two.

proof_stages.label: Rigidity benchmarks for smooth images of submanifolds
proof_stages.summary: Known rigidity results are recalled: smooth hypersurface or coisotropic images under symplectic homeomorphisms remain coisotropic and carry the corresponding characteristic foliation, with the Lagrangian case linked to Gromov-type rigidity.

proof_stages.label: Quantitative h-principle for C^0-small ambient flows
proof_stages.summary: A quantitative h-principle is formulated, upgrading a small homotopy between symplectic disks to an ambient Hamiltonian flow whose trajectories are uniformly small throughout the surrounding space.

proof_stages.label: High-dimensional submanifold flexibility
proof_stages.summary: Using the quantitative h-principle iteratively, one obtains high-dimensional symplectic homeomorphisms that change the symplectic area of a disk and, via a subcritical isotropic version, can even send symplectic disks to isotropic disks.

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian homeomorphisms and the Arnold fixed-point framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk shifts to fixed points, relating Poincaré--Birkhoff and surface Arnold-type results to the C^0 closure definition of Hamiltonian homeomorphisms and to the stricter notion of continuous Hamiltonian flows with convergent Hamiltonians.

proof_stages.label: One-fixed-point flexibility via curve h-principle
proof_stages.summary: A high-dimensional theorem is stated and sketched: a closed symplectic manifold admits a Hamiltonian homeomorphism with only the unavoidable single fixed point, constructed using a curve-level quantitative h-principle, invariant trees, and successive C^0-small perturbations avoiding new fixed points.

proof_stages.label: Open regularity, periodic-point, and C^0-structure questions
proof_stages.summary: The final discussion identifies remaining problems about Hölder or other regularity of area-changing symplectic homeomorphisms, periodic-point analogues of the fixed-point flexibility theorem, and possible rigidity or pathology for continuous symplectic structures.

examples.name: Poincaré--Birkhoff annulus theorem
examples.note: The annulus fixed-point theorem for an area-preserving homeomorphism with opposite boundary twists is used as historical and conceptual motivation for C^0 Hamiltonian fixed-point questions, not as an ingredient in the later constructions.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker states the Poincaré--Birkhoff annulus theorem and later refers back to its role, but does not work through an annulus example or derive the fixed points

examples.name: S^4 as a possible C^0 symplectic manifold
examples.note: The question whether S^4 could admit a topological atlas with symplectic-homeomorphism transitions serves as a concrete probe of how far C^0 symplectic flexibility might violate smooth symplectic obstructions.
examples.engagement_reason: S^4 is raised as a possible C^0 symplectic manifold and discussed as an open question, without checking or constructing any structure on it
